name: Gautama Buddha
title: Gautama Buddha - The Awakened One
description: Siddhartha Gautama (c. 563-483 BCE), who achieved perfect enlightenment under the Bodhi Tree and spent 45 years teaching the path to liberation from Samsara. The historical Buddha represents the potential for awakening present in all sentient beings.
summary: The Awakened One, Teacher of the Dharma. Founder of Buddhism who discovered the Middle Way and taught the Four Noble Truths and Noble Eightfold Path to liberation from suffering.

mudras.name: Bhumisparsha Mudra
mudras.meaning: Earth-touching gesture - calling the earth goddess to witness his enlightenment

mudras.name: Dharmachakra Mudra
mudras.meaning: Teaching gesture - setting the wheel of dharma in motion

mudras.name: Dhyana Mudra
mudras.meaning: Meditation gesture - deep contemplation and concentration

mudras.name: Abhaya Mudra
mudras.meaning: Fearlessness gesture - protection and dispelling fear

physicalMarks: Elongated earlobes - renunciation of royal jewelry
32 major marks and 80 minor marks of a great being

temples: Mahabodhi Temple, Bodh Gaya - site of enlightenment

sources.content: You yourselves must strive; the Buddhas only point the way. Those who meditate and enter the path are freed from the bonds of Mara.
Before my enlightenment, while I was still an unenlightened Bodhisatta, I too, being subject to birth, sought what was also subject to birth...
Be islands unto yourselves, refuges unto yourselves, seeking no external refuge; with the Dhamma as your island, the Dhamma as your refuge.
This is the Noble Truth of suffering: birth is suffering, aging is suffering, illness is suffering, death is suffering...
Account of the Buddha's discovery of the Middle Way and rejection of extreme austerities

sources.author: Ashvaghosa
sources.content: Epic poem describing Buddha's life from birth to parinirvana
The Tathagata has been constantly in this Saha world preaching the Dharma and teaching beings. My life-span is immeasurable, incalculable kalpas.

sources.author: Gampopa
sources.content: All sentient beings possess the essence of the perfect Buddha in themselves - Buddha-nature exists within all beings.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Siddhartha Gautama was born into a royal family in Lumbini (present-day Nepal) around 563 BCE. Prophecies foretold he would become either a great king or a great spiritual leader. His father, determined to make him a king, shielded him from suffering. Siddhartha lived a life of luxury and privilege, unaware of the realities of old age, sickness, and death. However, during chariot rides outside the palace, he encountered an old man, a sick man, a corpse, and an ascetic. These encounters profoundly impacted him, leading him to question the meaning of life and the nature of suffering. At the age of 29, he renounced his royal life, leaving his wife and son to embark on a quest for enlightenment. This event is known as the Great Renunciation.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: After his renunciation, Siddhartha spent years as an ascetic, practicing extreme self-denial and meditation. He studied with various teachers but found no lasting answers. Realizing that self-mortification was not the path to enlightenment, he abandoned asceticism and began to nourish himself. He sat under a Bodhi tree in Bodh Gaya, vowing not to move until he attained enlightenment. Mara, the demon of illusion, tempted him with visions of pleasure, power, and fear, but Siddhartha remained steadfast. Through deep meditation, he overcame these temptations and attained enlightenment, becoming the Buddha, the Awakened One. His first sermon, delivered at Sarnath, set in motion the Wheel of Dharma, outlining the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path, the foundations of Buddhist teachings.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Buddhism is not typically considered a theistic religion in the same way as others, the Buddha is revered as a supreme teacher and an embodiment of enlightenment. Devotees express their reverence through various practices, including meditation, chanting, prostration, and the offering of flowers, incense, and light. Buddhist temples and monasteries serve as centers for worship, study, and community. Images and statues of the Buddha are common, serving as focal points for meditation and reminders of his qualities. The veneration of relics associated with the Buddha and other enlightened beings is also a significant aspect of Buddhist practice. The goal is not to worship the Buddha as a god, but to emulate his example and follow his path to liberation.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Buddha's teachings have profoundly influenced philosophy, ethics, and culture across Asia and beyond. Buddhism has spread to various countries, adapting to local customs and traditions while maintaining its core principles. Different schools of Buddhism, such as Theravada, Mahayana, and Vajrayana, have emerged, each with its unique interpretations and practices. The Buddha's emphasis on compassion, mindfulness, and non-violence has resonated with people of all backgrounds. In the modern world, Buddhist principles are increasingly applied to address issues such as stress, anxiety, and environmental concerns. The Buddha's legacy continues to inspire individuals to cultivate wisdom, compassion, and inner peace.

birth.description: Born as Prince Siddhartha Gautama in Lumbini (present-day Nepal) around 563 BCE to King Suddhodana of the Shakya clan and Queen Maya.
birth.prophecy: The sage Asita prophesied he would become either a great king (chakravartin) or a great spiritual teacher (buddha).
birth.miracleSigns: Seven steps at birth, lotus flowers blooming, proclamation 'I am chief in the world'

fourSights: An Old Man - revealing the inevitability of aging
A Sick Person - showing the reality of disease and suffering
A Corpse - demonstrating the certainty of death
A Wandering Ascetic - displaying serene dignity and possibility of transcendence
biography.greatRenunciation: At age 29, on the night of his son Rahula's birth, Siddhartha left the palace, cutting his hair with his sword, exchanging royal garments for simple robes.
biography.asceticYears: Six years of extreme austerities with five companions, studying with teachers Alara Kalama and Uddaka Ramaputta, before realizing the Middle Way.

enlightenment.location: Bodh Gaya, under the Bodhi Tree
enlightenment.duration: One night of the full moon in May (Vesak)
threeWatches: First Watch: Recalled all previous lives across countless eons
Second Watch: Perceived the death and rebirth of all beings according to karma
Third Watch: Comprehended the Four Noble Truths and Dependent Origination
enlightenment.maraDefeat: Defeated Mara's armies and temptations by touching the earth (Bhumisparsha), calling Earth Goddess Prithvi to witness his countless lifetimes of virtue

biography.firstTeaching: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta (Setting in Motion the Wheel of Dharma) at the Deer Park in Sarnath, teaching the Middle Way, Four Noble Truths, and Noble Eightfold Path
biography.parinirvana: At age 80 in Kushinagar, lying between two sal trees in the lion's posture, giving final instructions: 'All conditioned things are subject to decay. Strive with diligence!'

jatakaTales: Prince Vessantara - the perfection of generosity
King Shibi - sacrificing flesh to save a dove
The Compassionate Elephant - giving his body to starving travelers
The Six-Tusked Elephant - forgiveness of the hunter

fourNobleTruths: Magga (Path) - the Noble Eightfold Path leads to cessation
threeMarksOfExistence: Anicca (Impermanence) - all conditioned phenomena are in constant flux
Dukkha (Unsatisfactoriness) - all conditioned phenomena are ultimately unsatisfying
Anatta (Non-self) - no permanent, unchanging self exists
coreTeachings.dependentOrigination: The chain of 12 links (nidanas) explaining how suffering arises and ceases through causation

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Represents enlightenment, wisdom, and the radiant nature of Buddhahood.

associations.name: Lion
associations.significance: Symbolizes royalty, strength, and the fearless proclamation of the Dharma.

associations.name: Dharma Wheel
associations.significance: Represents the teachings of the Buddha and the path to enlightenment.

associations.name: Eight
associations.significance: Associated with the Noble Eightfold Path, the core principles of Buddhist practice.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Represents clarity, illumination, and the dispelling of ignorance.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Symbolizes purity, fluidity, and the ability to adapt to all circumstances.

associations.name: Elephant
associations.significance: Symbolizes mental strength, steadfastness, and the power of mindfulness.

worshipPractices.name: Meditation
worshipPractices.description: The practice of training the mind to achieve a state of calm awareness and insight. Different forms of meditation are practiced to develop mindfulness, concentration, compassion, and wisdom. Meditation is central to Buddhist practice, as it is the method by which the Buddha himself achieved enlightenment.

worshipPractices.name: Puja
worshipPractices.description: Rituals of devotion and offering, often involving chanting, prostrations, and the offering of flowers, incense, and other symbolic items. Puja is performed to express gratitude, reverence, and to accumulate merit. It serves as a way to connect with the Buddha and the Buddhist teachings.

festivals.name: Buddha Purnima (Vesak)
festivals.timing: Full moon of the Vaisakha month (April/May)
festivals.description: Celebrates the birth, enlightenment, and death of the Buddha. Marked by prayer, meditation, almsgiving, and the release of animals. It is a time for reflection on the Buddha's teachings and recommitment to the path of Dharma.

festivals.name: Magha Puja Day
festivals.timing: Full moon of the Magha month (January/February)
festivals.description: Commemorates the spontaneous gathering of 1,250 of the Buddha's disciples. Celebrated with chanting, meditation, and acts of service. It emphasizes the importance of community and the principles of Buddhist practice.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Buddha's teachings continue to influence millions worldwide, promoting peace, compassion, and mindfulness. Buddhist principles are increasingly integrated into secular fields like psychology, healthcare, and education, contributing to stress reduction, emotional well-being, and ethical decision-making. His teachings on non-violence have inspired social justice movements globally.

shortDescription: The enlightened one and founder of Buddhism. He taught the path to liberation from suffering and the cycle of rebirth.
subtitle: The Awakened One: Teacher of Gods and Humans
